feedback.text: Hello, Firstly, I'd like to say that I completely support the Digital Fairness Act and its aim to rein in on the bad practices that we've had to deal with for years and years online. Thank you for your efforts regarding these issues. However, I also think that the Digital Fairness Act could be expanded to include policies that prevent video game publishers from destroying and taking away video games that have already been sold to customers. I have been playing video games for more than 20 years, and I think that the situation today regarding this medium is alarming. A substantial number of games that I've played throughout my life are now completely unavailable and have disappeared. Games that I've bought with my own money and that have been removed or rendered inaccessible by the publishers. I couldn't say the same thing for the movies, books, and music that I grew up with. That's because video game publishers have profited for years and years from this unregulated market and lack of legal ground which allowed them to treat video games as a commodity that can be removed and taken away as they so desire. By doing this, publishers not only remove a product that has been sold from the hands of a customer, but they also destroy works of art and remove them from being available to future generations. Video games are a young art form, and I feel that their cultural significance and value have only been highlighted in the past few years. If you draw a parallel with film and music, think how devastating it is to the cultural knowledge to have lost a whole portion of the movies from the silent era or the musical master recordings in fires, works that won't be replaced and that we won't get to learn from. Imagine now that it's happening right now to video games because we let it take place, even though it's entirely preventable. Video games are a valuable part of our cultural history, and it's a medium that deserves to be studied and enjoyed by current and future generations, and that is why its preservation is important. I do think that the Digital Fairness Act could change the state of this issue by protecting customers and making sure that what they've bought isn't taken away from them and destroyed. Thank you.
